problem_summary: 2012 MCM Problem C (ICM, interdisciplinary): the classic "conspiracy / crime busting" data problem. Given a company of ~83 office workers, message traffic among them, a list of 15 discussion topics (some flagged suspicious), and a partial ground truth (7 known conspirators, 8 known non-conspirators), the team must (1) prioritize all remaining workers by likelihood of being a conspirator and identify the gang leader; (2) re-rank when new clues appear (a previously-innocent topic becomes suspicious and Chris becomes a conspirator); and (3) show how semantic/text analysis of message content would refine the model. The team validated against the small "EZ" 10-person example before scaling to the full 83-node network.
models_used: Social Network Analysis (SNA) on a graph of workers (nodes) and messages (edges) — the core framework

Their own 'Expanding Criminal Network Analysis Model' (ECNAM): a two-level score combining a node-level Centrality term and a link-level Criminal Information Content (CIC) term, final score S(v) = Centrality(v) · CIC(v)
Freeman (1979) centrality measures: normalized Degree d_d, Betweenness d_b, Closeness d_c, combined as d_s(v) = (d_d + d_b)/d_c

Criminal Information Content (CIC): a per-edge weighted sum W_1 = w_t·f(v) + w_c·g(v) + w_d·h(v) over topic-suspiciousness f, conversation-object identity g (ternary 1/0.5/0 for conspirator/unknown/non-conspirator), and message direction h (1 outgoing / 0.5 incoming), summed over all incident edges
Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP, Saaty) to set the CIC metric weights — pairwise comparison matrix giving w = [0.6, 0.3, 0.1] for topic/conversationalist/direction with a consistency check (CI≈0)
Fisher's Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) as a competing/benchmark classifier (degree, topic, conversation-object features), explicitly compared against ECNAM to argue superiority

A simple text/semantic-analysis refinement: decomposing topics into 4 binary feature indicators (economic situation, safety measures, known conspirators, Spanish-as-codeword) and recomputing edge weight c_1 = Σ k_i·i_i with equal weights k_i = 0.25, producing graded topic weights instead of binary suspicious/not
Score normalization to 0-100 (S_final = 100·S/S_max) and a 'max-slope-change' cut-off rule to split conspirators from innocents
Leader detection via a separate Lead_detect_score = Degree + Betweenness + Closeness on the induced subgraph of identified conspirators
key_techniques: Decomposing the problem into two orthogonal axes — node-level (who is structurally central) and link-level (how much suspicious information they carry) — and multiplying them, so a person needs BOTH high connectivity AND suspicious content to score high. This directly maps the four-quadrant intuition (both low → innocent; high centrality but low CIC → social but clean; etc.)

Reframing every worker as a 'suspicious conspirator' with a probability in [0,1]: known conspirators = 1, known innocents = 0, unknowns in between — an elegant way to fold partial ground truth into a continuous ranking ('straight line as a special curve' analogy)

Encoding directionality and conversation-partner identity into the edge weights (g ternary, h for in/out degree) — extending classic undirected SNA to capture that talking suspicious topics actively, and with a known conspirator, is more incriminating
Using AHP to justify otherwise-arbitrary weights with a defensible, auditable procedure plus a consistency check, rather than hand-waving the numbers

Validation-first workflow: testing on the tiny EZ case (recovering Dave/George as known + matching the supervisor's expert judgment, including agreeing on the misjudged borderline cases) before trusting results on the full 83-node network

Sensitivity / scenario analysis as first-class deliverables: re-running with Gretchen-as-manager, with the new-clue scenario, and with/without text analysis, and reporting how the conspirator count and leader change
Head-to-head benchmarking against Fisher LDA to argue ECNAM is better (ranks instead of just classifies; works on small samples where LDA failed on EZ; uses network structure not just statistics)

Reporting a concrete, stable misjudgment rate (~20%) and naming exactly who is misclassified (Paige as false positive; Ulf, Harvey as false negatives) — turning error into an honest, quantified caveat

Graph-theoretic 'depth analysis' of the recovered conspirator subgraph (degree>5 for all, shortest paths of length 1-2, hierarchy signal) to characterize conspiracy behavior, going beyond just producing a list
why_it_won: From a judge's view this paper wins on the things Problem C rewards: a custom, well-motivated model (ECNAM) rather than an off-the-shelf classifier, and a clear narrative for WHY it is built that way. The node×link decomposition is genuinely insightful and the four-quadrant interpretation makes the model intuitive and defensible. Crucially the team validated on the EZ example first and showed agreement with the official 'supervisor' judgment (including the subtle borderline cases), which signals modeling maturity. They didn't stop at requirement 1 — they did the new-clue re-ranking, the text-analysis refinement, a leader-detection sub-model, and a depth analysis of conspirator behavior, covering every prompt and then some. They benchmarked against Fisher LDA and argued superiority concretely (ranking vs. classifying, robustness on small samples). They quantified and named their errors (20% misjudgment, specific people) instead of hiding them, and they generalized the method into a reusable 'statistics → custom indices → weighted score' recipe at the end. The combination — original model + validation + full requirement coverage + honest error reporting + benchmark + generalization — is exactly the Outstanding profile.
section_highlights: The strongest structural moves: (1) The Section 4 build-up is textbook — Analysis of Question → why prior models don't fit (a crisp bulleted list of four mismatches: mixed suspect/innocent population, directed edges, heterogeneous edge meaning, node importance depending on links) → then ECNAM as the fix. Motivating the model by enumerating gaps in the literature is very persuasive to judges. (2) A clean Notations/Symbols table up front and consistent symbols throughout. (3) The four-quadrant taxonomy (centrality-low/high × CIC-low/high) gives readers an immediate mental model of what the score means. (4) Validation (EZ case) placed before the full result, establishing trust. (5) A dedicated Fisher-vs-ECNAM comparison section that uses a competitor to make its own case. (6) Section 7 'depth analysis' with the conspirator-subgraph visualizations (node size/color = score), leader-detection table, and per-topic activity indices — strong, concrete figures. (7) Section 7.2 distills a 3-step general-purpose method, and Section 8 gives an honest, specific Strengths/Weaknesses/Future-work close.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Sloppy error-rate reporting that would worry a careful judge: the abstract and EZ section say 'misjudgment rate 20%' but the EZ text literally writes 'The error rate is 80%' (likely a typo for 80% accuracy / 20% error) — the numbers contradict themselves in places

Internal inconsistency in headline results: the abstract says 23 conspirators originally then 28 after new clues ('21.7% more'), but the new-clues section says the number 'reduces to 28 while the original is 23' (28 is an increase, not a reduction), and the text-analysis section reports yet another count (18, or 16 without text analysis with a >50% cutoff). The conspirator count is never pinned down consistently

The two-level score S = Centrality · CIC is asserted ('we define the product') with no real justification for multiplication vs. weighted sum, and Centrality itself uses an unusual (d_d + d_b)/d_c form with closeness in the denominator that is never derived or sanity-checked

AHP is slightly internally inconsistent: stated raw scores are 6/3/1 but the comparison matrix uses 2 and 3 (and 6) and yields w=[0.6,0.3,0.1]; the 'CI=0 so CR=0' claim is overstated for a 3×3 matrix and reads as glossing the consistency check

The text-analysis 'semantic' refinement is shallow — it never actually processes message text (the team admits the original messages aren't available); it just re-labels the 15 given topics with 4 hand-set binary features and equal 0.25 weights, so the 'Semantic/Text Analysis' framing oversells what was done

The OCR/markdown rendering shows garbled figures (Figure 4 table degenerates into dozens of '0 / Yale' rows; mermaid node lists are clearly mis-parsed name soup), so the actual figure quality can't be fully judged from this file, but the underlying name lists look noisy/duplicated (multiple 'Beth', 'Marion', 'ChristinaBariol') suggesting messy data handling

Leader identity is unstable across sub-models: the main model names Elsie as leader, but the conspirator-subgraph leader-detection names Sherri (with Elsie a close second). The paper doesn't fully reconcile why the leader changes between the two analyses
Equation (4-8) defines c_1 = k_1 i_1 + k_2 i_2 + k_3 i_3 (three terms) while the text describes four features i_1..i_4 and four weights — the fourth term is dropped in the formula

The cut-off rule ('point where slope changes maximum') is heuristic and admittedly lacks a principled threshold — flagged in their own Weaknesses, and it is what drives the wobbling conspirator counts
English/grammar is rough throughout (typical of the era's translated submissions); doesn't block comprehension but a judge notices the lack of polish
reusable_patterns: The node×link two-factor score: build a per-node structural term (centrality) and a per-node content/behavior term (information carried), then combine — reusable for any 'find the important/suspicious actors in a network' problem (influencers, fraud, supply-chain risk)

Fold partial ground truth into a continuous index by assigning known-positive=1, known-negative=0, unknown∈(0,1), then rank everyone on one scale — clean way to handle MCM problems that give you labeled exemplars

AHP for weight-setting when you must justify subjective coefficients: state pairwise importance, build the matrix, take the principal eigenvector, report a consistency check. Cheap, auditable, judge-friendly
Validate on the provided toy/example case first, and explicitly check agreement with any 'expert/supervisor' answer the prompt gives, before reporting full-scale results — buys enormous credibility

Benchmark your custom model against a standard method (here Fisher LDA) and argue superiority on specific, named dimensions (ranking vs. classifying, small-sample robustness, use of structure) rather than asserting it
Treat scenario/sensitivity analysis (new clues, an extra suspect, with/without a refinement) as required deliverables and report deltas in the key output (count, leader, ranks)
Quantify error honestly and name the specific misclassified entities — a reported, stable error rate with named false positives/negatives reads as confidence, not weakness

Close by abstracting the solution into a short general-purpose recipe ('statistics of network properties → transform into node/edge indices → weighted-average score → identify/prioritize/categorize') to demonstrate transferability beyond the prompt
Encode edge semantics (direction, partner identity, topic type) into edge weights to extend plain undirected SNA when the problem has directed/heterogeneous links
Use a small interpretability device — here the 4-quadrant low/high × low/high taxonomy — to make a composite score immediately understandable to readers and judges
